milestone.name: Gold-standard foundation (2026 H1)
milestone.description: Standards-first hardening of Coding Harness across product clarity, AI governance, supply chain security, release integrity, and trust artifacts.

update_existing.title: Define the Coding Harness core command spine and publish a standards-aligned product contract
update_existing.description:
## Summary

Define the smallest stable product surface for Coding Harness and publish one authoritative product contract for the repo.

## Why

Coding Harness is strong but still easier to respect than to explain. The command surface is broad enough that the core can get blurred by adjacent tooling. The repo needs a single, stable definition of:

- what the harness is
- what commands are core
- what commands are integrations
- what commands are adjacent/experimental
- which external control families the core helps satisfy

## Standards alignment

This issue should make the repo legible against:

- NIST SP 800-218 (SSDF v1.1)
- NIST SP 800-218A
- NIST AI RMF 1.0
- NIST AI 600-1 Generative AI Profile
- OpenSSF OSPS Baseline
- CISA Secure by Design
- SLSA

## Deliverables

- Add a short product contract near the top of `README.md`
- Add a `Core / Integration / Adjacent` command-family map
- Add a standards map from command families to the external control families above
- Add maturity levels for major command families: `stable`, `beta`, `experimental`
- Ensure CLI help text and docs use the same taxonomy

## Acceptance criteria

- A new maintainer can explain Coding Harness in under 2 minutes
- README, CLI help, and project docs tell the same architecture story
- Each major core command family maps to at least one explicit external control family
- Core commands are visibly separate from adjacent utilities

update_existing.title: Refactor `ci-migrate` into a secure release and deployment control subsystem
update_existing.description:
## Summary

Break `ci-migrate` into explicit submodules so it reads as a secure release and deployment control subsystem rather than one oversized command file.

## Why

`ci-migrate` is one of the heaviest surfaces in the repo and sits at the intersection of release safety, provenance, rollback, branch protection, and deployment trust.

## Suggested split

- snapshot capture and restore
- parity verification
- proof-pack generation
- branch-protection and merge-queue integration
- break-glass policy and approvals
- apply / promote / commit orchestration
- deployment evidence capture

## Standards alignment

Design notes should explain how the subsystem supports:

- CISA Safe Software Deployment
- GitHub Actions hardening
- SLSA provenance expectations
- rollback and release evidence

## Acceptance criteria

- Top-level `ci-migrate` command file becomes an orchestration shell
- Rollback, parity, and proof-pack flows are independently testable
- Submodules map to clear release/deployment responsibilities
- The subsystem has a documented control story, not just command behavior

update_existing.title: Slim `src/cli.ts` to a policy-neutral shell with dispatch and error normalization only
update_existing.description:
## Summary

Reduce `src/cli.ts` to a thin shell responsible only for argument parsing, dispatch, help/version output, output mode selection, and top-level error normalization.

## Why

The CLI entrypoint should not be an architecture hotspot. Command behavior belongs in command modules and shared libraries.

## Acceptance criteria

- Command behavior lives outside `src/cli.ts`
- New commands can be added without materially increasing CLI-shell complexity
- Exit codes and JSON output are normalized consistently across command families

update_existing.title: Split `init` scaffold generation by managed surface and generated evidence
update_existing.description:
## Summary

Refactor `init` scaffolding so generation logic is grouped by what it produces, and add golden-output fixtures for each generated surface.

## Why

`init` is effectively the repo substrate compiler. Its outputs should be independently understandable and testable.

## Suggested split

- contract artifacts
- workflow artifacts
- CI / automation artifacts
- scripts and local wrappers
- docs and examples
- migration and update helpers

## Acceptance criteria

- `init` stays as orchestration, not generation monolith
- Generated surfaces can be tested independently
- Dry-run, track, update, migrate, and rollback remain stable
- Example outputs become reusable documentation inputs

update_existing.title: Publish verifiable trust artifacts and reference examples for the harness
update_existing.description:
## Summary

Publish small, inspectable example artifacts for the main trust surfaces so users can verify what the harness produces without running the full system.

## Why

The repo makes strong claims around governance, evidence, parity, rollback, and rollout control. Those claims need inspectable reference outputs.

## Publish examples for

- `harness.contract.json`
- generated `WORKFLOW.md`
- gate-result JSON
- run-record / evidence artifact
- pilot decision packet
- CI migration proof-pack
- SBOM in SPDX 3.x or CycloneDX 1.7
- build provenance attestation
- npm provenance example for package releases

## Acceptance criteria

- A reader can inspect trust outputs directly from the repo
- Examples are linked from README
- Examples are current enough to serve as real references

update_existing.title: Harden the workflow-contract subsystem around explicit trust, safety, and AI-governance boundaries
update_existing.description:
## Summary

Tighten the workflow-contract subsystem so it is a clearly public architectural core with explicit safety, trust, and AI-governance boundaries.

## Why

This subsystem is more than `WORKFLOW.md` support. It should align with secure development and AI risk-management guidance, not just internal parsing.

## Clarify and separate

- parser
- normalization
- semantic validation
- artifact registry
- scorecard generation
- policy/gate binding

## Standards alignment

Add a control map from workflow constructs to:

- NIST SP 800-218A
- NIST AI RMF 1.0
- NIST AI 600-1 Generative AI Profile

## Acceptance criteria

- The workflow-contract model is a public subsystem, not hidden implementation detail
- Safety- and trust-related checks are explicit and documented
- Adding a workflow rule does not require coupling to unrelated command paths

update_existing.title: Clarify `pilot-evaluate` as a measurable AI-governance decision engine
update_existing.description:
## Summary

Tighten `pilot-evaluate` so its evaluation path, thresholds, evidence, and decisions are easy to follow and machine-auditable.

## Why

This subsystem answers a high-stakes question: whether autonomy can safely expand. It should read as a measure/manage control loop, not just a report generator.

## Separate and document

- input collection
- threshold evaluation
- lane-specific policies
- decision packet generation
- reporting/output formatting

## Standards alignment

Align language and outputs with:

- NIST AI RMF `Measure` and `Manage`
- CISA Secure by Design emphasis on evidence and operational safety

## Acceptance criteria

- The evaluation path is traceable end to end
- Thresholds and decisions are machine-auditable
- Decision packets are readable by someone who did not author the code

update_existing.title: Sequence the architecture backlog as a standards-first hardening program
update_existing.description:
## Summary

Define the execution order for the current architecture, AI governance, supply-chain, and trust-artifact backlog so work lands in the right sequence.

## Suggested sequence

1. JSC-103 core product contract
2. JSC-105 CLI shell
3. JSC-106 init split
4. JSC-104 ci-migrate split
5. JSC-108 workflow-contract hardening
6. JSC-109 pilot-evaluate clarification
7. JSC-111 release provenance and trusted publishing
8. JSC-112 OSPS / Scorecard baseline
9. JSC-113 AI assistant security policy
10. JSC-114 SLSA roadmap
11. JSC-115 GitHub Actions hardening
12. JSC-116 Secure by Design / safe deployment posture
13. JSC-107 trust-artifact examples

## Acceptance criteria

- The order is explicit in project planning
- Each later issue depends on earlier clarified architecture
- Docs and examples are written after the underlying architecture stabilizes

create_new.title: Implement release provenance, signed attestations, and trusted publishing for Coding Harness
create_new.description:
## Summary

Adopt modern release-integrity controls so consumers can verify where and how Coding Harness releases were built and published.

## Deliverables

- Use GitHub artifact attestations for release outputs
- Adopt GitHub Actions OIDC where possible instead of long-lived secrets
- Adopt npm trusted publishing
- Publish npm packages with provenance
- Add verification steps to release docs and CI
- Store example verification commands for consumers

## Acceptance criteria

- Release artifacts have verifiable provenance
- npm releases use trusted publishing, not long-lived write tokens
- Consumers can verify release provenance from docs alone
- Release workflows follow least-privilege token permissions

create_new.title: Adopt OpenSSF OSPS Baseline and Scorecard as the measurable minimum security posture
create_new.description:
## Summary

Declare and track a public, current baseline for the repo's security posture.

## Deliverables

- Select a target OSPS Baseline level for the repo
- Create a control matrix mapping repo settings and docs to OSPS controls
- Run OpenSSF Scorecard regularly
- Fail or warn on regressions in key Scorecard checks
- Add visible status references once criteria are met

## Acceptance criteria

- The repo declares a target OSPS level and current status
- Scorecard runs continuously
- Gaps are visible and tracked instead of informal

create_new.title: Establish a secure AI assistant and agent instruction policy for the repo
create_new.description:
## Summary

Add one authoritative security-focused AI assistant instruction policy for the repo and align harness-managed skills/prompts to it.

## Deliverables

- Add a repo-level AI assistant instruction file / policy
- Align it with the OpenSSF Security-Focused Guide for AI Code Assistant Instructions
- Include dependency-selection, secret-handling, test-generation, logging, provenance, and workflow-generation rules
- Align harness-generated agent instructions / skills with the same policy

## Acceptance criteria

- The repo contains one authoritative security-focused instruction policy for AI assistants
- Harness-managed prompts / skills do not drift from the policy
- The policy explicitly addresses hallucinated dependencies, secrets, secure defaults, and security testing

create_new.title: Create a SLSA v1.2 roadmap for source integrity and build integrity
create_new.description:
## Summary

Publish a realistic target-state and gap analysis for Coding Harness against SLSA v1.2.

## Deliverables

- Document current and target SLSA levels for source and build tracks
- Map branch protection, review rules, provenance, and build isolation to those levels
- Identify which controls are repo responsibilities vs platform responsibilities
- Publish a `current / next / later` roadmap

## Acceptance criteria

- Consumers can understand the project's current SLSA posture
- Source and build integrity controls are not conflated
- Two-party review, provenance, and build isolation are tracked explicitly

create_new.title: Harden GitHub Actions and repository policy to a least-privilege default
create_new.description:
## Summary

Make the repo model safe automation by default, following GitHub's current guidance for Actions security.

## Deliverables

- Pin third-party actions to immutable SHAs
- Set explicit minimal `permissions:` in workflows
- Protect workflow files with CODEOWNERS / review policy
- Review self-hosted runner usage and document trust boundaries
- Add checks for risky workflow patterns

## Acceptance criteria

- All third-party actions are pinned or justified
- Workflow token permissions are explicit and minimal
- Changes to workflows receive stronger review than ordinary code
- Runner trust model is documented

create_new.title: Publish a Secure by Design and safe-deployment posture for Coding Harness
create_new.description:
## Summary

Publish a concise Secure by Design posture and a safe deployment / rollback playbook for the project.

## Deliverables

- Publish a concise Secure by Design posture statement
- Publish a safe deployment / rollback playbook
- Define logging / evidence expectations for deployment and rollback events
- Define how Coding Harness reduces classes of workflow or policy failure at scale

## Acceptance criteria

- Security posture is publicly stated in outcome terms, not just implementation terms
- Deployment and rollback are guided by documented playbooks
- Logs/evidence for release and rollback are part of the standard process
